title: Sword Of Destiny

jokes.text: Narrator undercuts Michael's claim about blueprints: 'Well, that part wasn't true. But they would've.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells a client they already have blueprints drawn up to justify the contract
jokes.punchline: Narrator immediately and matter-of-factly reveals this is a lie, then charitably adds 'But they would've'
jokes.explanation: The Arrested Development narrator is one of TV's great comedic devices — deadpan omniscience used to immediately puncture character pretension. The 'But they would've' kicker is perfectly economical, softening the brutal fact-check with an absurd defense of Michael that makes it funnier. Classic show DNA.

jokes.text: Lindsay reveals she needs a break from Mrs. Featherbottom — who IS Tobias — and wants Michael to hire him so she gets relief from her own husband in disguise
jokes.setup: Lindsay calls Michael urgently about hiring Tobias
jokes.punchline: Her motivation is that Tobias's disguise as her housekeeper is exhausting, so she wants the real Tobias employed elsewhere — the absurdity of her not simply revealing the disguise is the joke
jokes.explanation: Earns high character integration because only Lindsay, defined by laziness and avoidance, would prefer to navigate this insane situation rather than simply confront it. The logic is airtight within the show's world, which is what elevates it above cheap absurdism.

jokes.text: 'No. She's the only one who can hand wash my delicates.' / 'Yeah, the real Tobias wouldn't do that.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks why Lindsay doesn't just tell Tobias she knows he's Mrs. Featherbottom
jokes.punchline: Lindsay won't expose the ruse because the disguised Tobias performs domestic tasks the real Tobias wouldn't — implying she prefers the compliant housekeeper version of her husband
jokes.explanation: This is a compact, devastating joke about the Tobias-Lindsay marriage. She prefers the fiction of a dutiful husband to the reality. The Michael follow-up ('Yeah, the real Tobias wouldn't do that') landing as agreement rather than outrage is perfect straight-man work. High quotability, strong rewatch value.

jokes.text: Tobias produces a résumé from 'Gobias Industries' — pronounced with a hard G — which stands for 'Go buy us a cup of coffee'
jokes.setup: Tobias submits a résumé for Michael's assistant job from his own company, 'Gobias Industries'
jokes.punchline: The company name is a phonetic gag: 'Go buy us' — it's a fake company whose name describes the most menial task Tobias would assign
jokes.explanation: A genuinely brilliant wordplay construction. 'Gobias' sounds like a real name (even an exotic one), the hard-G correction adds a beat of confusion, and the reveal that it dissolves into a coffee errand is a perfect misdirection payoff. It also works as character comedy — Tobias would absolutely name his company this. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: 'Booyah!' — Tobias pulls out the résumé, already anticipating Michael would ask for one
jokes.setup: Michael stalls by saying Tobias would need to submit a résumé
jokes.punchline: Tobias immediately produces one with a triumphant 'Booyah!' — he came prepared for every objection, which is absurdly over-prepared
jokes.explanation: The 'Booyah' is perfectly Tobias — desperately enthusiastic and slightly off in its register. Works as character comedy and sets up the Gobias reveal nicely.

jokes.text: 'It looks like you might be a little too qualified' — Michael finds a way to reject Tobias after the Gobias reveal
jokes.setup: Tobias has just explained that Gobias Industries means 'go buy us a cup of coffee'
jokes.punchline: Michael flips the script — instead of saying Tobias is unqualified, he says 'too qualified,' a polite lie delivered straight
jokes.explanation: A competent ironic dodge — Michael's social discomfort with rejecting family members played straight. The irony is evident and gets a solid chuckle but isn't particularly novel.

jokes.text: Tobias appoints himself Michael's new assistant on the spot without being hired
jokes.setup: Michael tries to let Tobias down gently
jokes.punchline: Tobias ignores the rejection entirely and begins performing the job immediately, 'gung ho'
jokes.explanation: Tobias's obliviousness to social cues is a reliable character engine. The joke works but doesn't break new ground — we've seen Tobias barrel through rejection before.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals Starla was bitten by 'a well-known music producer's guard dog'
jokes.setup: Starla mentions she has rabies and is on day four of shots
jokes.punchline: The narrator's explanation is absurdly specific and celebrity-adjacent — the detail of it being a 'well-known music producer's guard dog' suggests a whole insane backstory delivered in one throwaway line
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator economy — a ridiculous situation unpacked in 10 words. The specificity ('well-known') implies celebrity without naming anyone, which is funnier than naming them.

jokes.text: Tobias ejects Ted from the office: 'No. Well, then, Ted can get the hell out of this office!' — then announces 'And that's how you keep out unwanted visitors'
jokes.setup: Michael just told Starla to keep unwanted visitors out; Tobias takes over as self-appointed assistant; Ted wanders in to discuss the lease
jokes.punchline: Tobias interprets his role with aggressive literalism and throws out Ted (a legitimate colleague) with great force, then takes a bow
jokes.explanation: The irony is rich: Tobias, an unwanted visitor himself, enforces anti-visitor policy. His self-satisfied bow after the ejection is perfect character comedy. The joke also accidentally removes a character who would have solved Michael's business problem, setting up the episode's thematic irony.

jokes.text: 'Let me take off my assistant skirt and put on my Barbra Streisand in The Prince of Tides ass-masking therapist pantsuit'
jokes.setup: Tobias wants to offer Michael psychological insight
jokes.punchline: His metaphor for switching roles involves an absurdly specific Barbra Streisand film reference combined with the phrase 'ass-masking' — the specificity of his fashion/therapy metaphor is the joke
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in Tobias-as-character. The line does several things simultaneously: references his failed acting career (Barbra Streisand), his abandoned therapy career, his body image issues (ass-masking), and his tendency to over-elaborate simple thoughts. 'Ass-masking therapist pantsuit' is one of the most quotable Tobias lines. Only Tobias Fünke could say this line — maximum character integration.

jokes.text: Lindsay mentions an Eastern medicine store called 'Ancient Chinese Secret' — both characters say the name simultaneously in a recognizing tone
jokes.setup: Lindsay recommends an Eastern medicine store
jokes.punchline: Both Michael and Lindsay say 'Ancient Chinese Secret, huh?' in unison — a reference to a classic Calgon laundry detergent ad, suggesting they share cultural touchstones in an absurd context
jokes.explanation: A pop culture callback that works if you know the reference. The simultaneous delivery is a nice character beat (siblings sharing a brain) but the joke leans on the reference rather than building on it.

jokes.text: Gob at Ancient Chinese Secret: 'Yeah, I need a tea to give my dingle less tingle'
jokes.setup: Narrator sets up Gob visiting the store 'but not for a migraine'
jokes.punchline: Gob's crude rhyming euphemism for a sexual health issue is delivered with complete confidence — 'dingle less tingle'
jokes.explanation: The rhyme 'dingle less tingle' is genuinely clever wordplay — it scans perfectly, conveys exactly what it needs to convey, and is delivered with Gob's characteristic bravado. High economy: four words do extraordinary work. Very quotable.

jokes.text: 'Me quick, want slow. Wait, that's Indian.'
jokes.setup: Gob is at a Chinese medicine store trying to explain his problem
jokes.punchline: He attempts broken Mandarin (or pidgin), then self-corrects realizing he's doing a different ethnic stereotype — delivered with zero self-awareness
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely on Gob — his oblivious racism is itself the punchline. The self-correction ('Wait, that's Indian') is what elevates it from a cringe ethnic joke to a character joke: he's not correcting the racism, just the geography. Very Gob.

jokes.text: Gob spots the Sword of Destiny and the store owner's explanation that it contains ancient magic — followed by the narrator's non-sequitur: 'Still, where'd the lighter fluid come from?'
jokes.setup: The store owner tells Gob the sword is full of ancient magic
jokes.punchline: Narrator cuts in with 'Still, where'd the lighter fluid come from?' — implying something flamable and inexplicable happened visually that was never addressed
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator joke — the narrator references something visual (lighter fluid appearing) that the subtitle-only transcript can't capture but the audience presumably sees. The 'Still' is key: it concedes the magic while questioning the pyrotechnics. High rewatch value because it rewards attention to the physical gag.

jokes.text: Narrator, about the flaming lighter fluid: 'Still, where'd the lighter fluid come from?'
jokes.setup: Gob has apparently produced flame from the sword (a visual gag inferred from context about his magic act).
jokes.punchline: The narrator himself can't account for this detail — breaking the fourth wall to note the show's own unexplained visual.
jokes.explanation: The narrator breaking to acknowledge a gap in his own omniscience is a sharp meta move. It suggests the show's world has absurdities even the narrator can't explain — which is both funny and philosophically consistent with AD's universe. The brevity is ideal.

jokes.text: Gob dismisses the ancient story of warning: 'Yeah, I make up my own patter. Just ring it up with the dong tea.'
jokes.setup: Store owner begins to tell the ancient story of warning that comes with the Sword of Destiny
jokes.punchline: Gob cuts off the portentous legend mid-sentence to order it like a coffee with a side of STD tea — 'dong tea' as a callback to the earlier tea request
jokes.explanation: Gob's imperviousness to ancient wisdom, his casual dismissal of legend, and the callback to 'dong tea' all fire simultaneously. The rhythm of 'ring it up with the dong tea' is perfect Gob cadence. Strong character integration — this is exactly how Gob would navigate a mystical artifact purchase.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Looks like you're looking for dragons, in the future.' — on seeing Gob with a sword
jokes.setup: Michael encounters Gob walking around with a large sword
jokes.punchline: Michael's response is a dry non-observation — he neither asks about it seriously nor makes a big deal of it, just offers a mild future-tense observation
jokes.explanation: A solid Michael deadpan. The 'in the future' tag is a slightly odd construction that works in a low-key way. Not exceptional but fits the episode's comedic rhythm.

jokes.text: Gob cuts himself on the Sword of Destiny immediately after being told to be careful; insists it's not blood; Michael confirms 'Yeah, that's blood.'
jokes.setup: Michael warns Gob to be careful with the sword
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately draws blood, denies it, and Michael matter-of-factly confirms it while Gob insists 'It'll get better before the show'
jokes.explanation: The foreshadowing is rich (the sword will cause real harm throughout the episode), and Gob's denial then shrug — 'It'll get better before the show' — is perfectly in character: he cannot acknowledge weakness even when bleeding.

jokes.text: 'Really picked up steam there at the end' — Michael's response to Gob's pitch ending with 'and you ram the Sword of Destiny into my belly'
jokes.setup: Gob pitches his comeback illusion scheme in detail
jokes.punchline: Michael's only response is to note the pitch's dramatic structure, not its insane content — treating 'ram a sword into my belly' as a logical conclusion to a business pitch
jokes.explanation: Beautiful economy. Michael's deadpan normalizes insanity by commenting on narrative arc rather than content. 'Picked up steam there at the end' is a writers'-room joke about pitch structure. High quotability.

jokes.text: 'I'll buy you 100 George Michaels you can teach to drive!' — Gob's offer as he loses blood
jokes.setup: Michael declines because he needs to teach George Michael to drive
jokes.punchline: Gob, apparently unaware that George Michael is a specific human child, offers to purchase 100 equivalent replacements
jokes.explanation: A stellar Gob line. The offer presupposes George Michael is a commodity and that 100 would compensate for one. It simultaneously comments on Gob's relationship with his nephew (doesn't register him as a person) and his financial grandiosity (while actively bleeding). Excellent economy and character specificity.

jokes.text: 'You're losing blood, aren't you?' 'Probably. My socks are wet.' — Gob's casual acknowledgment of potentially fatal blood loss
jokes.setup: Michael suspects Gob is seriously injured
jokes.punchline: Gob confirms it obliquely via the detail that his socks are wet — treating what could be life-threatening bleeding as a minor inconvenience
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'My socks are wet' as a diagnostic indicator of arterial blood loss is an extraordinarily economical comedic observation. The casualness is the joke — Gob is aware something is wrong but frames it in the most mundane possible terms. Comparable in quality to the calibration anchors provided. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: 'Sorry.' / 'You'll be sorry!' / [Beat] 'Wait, that doesn't work after his line.'
jokes.setup: Michael apologizes; Gob attempts a dramatic exit line
jokes.punchline: Gob realizes mid-threat that the dramatic retort doesn't grammatically or logically work as a response to 'sorry' — and says so out loud
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect joke. Gob's self-awareness about his own dramatic incompetence is funnier than the would-be threat itself. The meta-commentary — he analyzes the structural failure of his own exit line in real time — is a show-within-a-show joke about dramatic conventions. It's also a perfect Gob character moment: he cares more about performing well than about actual emotional authenticity. Among the best jokes in the episode; approaches the calibration anchor for a 9.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And Michael rushed home to teach his son to drive' — followed immediately by Michael explaining the stair car's quirks
jokes.setup: Michael plans to teach George Michael standard driving
jokes.punchline: The 'car' is the Bluth Company stair car — a comically unwieldy vehicle with hydraulic stairs, requiring instructions about 'two tons of stairs behind you' and 'hop-ons'
jokes.explanation: The stair car is one of the show's great running visual gags. Teaching driving on it adds a new comedic layer — all the 'normal' driving lesson conventions are warped by the vehicle's absurdity. 'Hop-on' as a technical term for people who grab the stairs is a great world-building joke.

jokes.text: Michael is immediately hit (presumably by the stair car steps) mid-lesson, the narrator commenting: 'And that's when Michael decided that perhaps it was time for a sick day'
jokes.setup: Michael is teaching George Michael about driving the stair car while insisting he doesn't need a sick day
jokes.punchline: He is immediately physically injured by the vehicle, leading to a hospital visit — the universe enforcing the sick day
jokes.explanation: The narrator's timing is the joke — 'decided that perhaps it was time for a sick day' is both understated and ironic, since Michael didn't decide anything, he was physically compelled. The universe-as-ironic-punisher is a recurring AD structural joke.

jokes.text: Michael gets hit by the stair car during the lesson. Narrator: 'And that's when Michael decided that perhaps it was time for a sick day.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been insisting he's never taken a sick day and is in control of everything.
jokes.punchline: He immediately gets injured by his own stair car — the narrator framing it as his 'decision' to take a sick day is the verbal payoff to the physical gag.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's framing of a painful accident as a polite 'decision' is a characteristic AD move. The structure is solid — the episode has been building toward this reversal of Michael's 'I never take sick days' claim. The irony lands well.

jokes.text: Mrs. Featherbottom (Tobias in disguise) arrives to nurse Michael: 'My baby is sick, but Mother is here to nurse you.' Michael: 'Well, now I'm a little sicker.'
jokes.setup: Michael is injured and being tended to
jokes.punchline: The sight of Mrs. Featherbottom/Tobias makes Michael feel worse rather than better — delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: Michael's deadpan response ('Well, now I'm a little sicker') is a clean, functional joke with good economy. The visual of the disguised Tobias in nurse mode adds physical comedy context.

jokes.text: Michael mid-sentence claiming to be in control: 'I'm in charge and I'm in con— I'm peeing. Am I peeing?'
jokes.setup: Michael insists the company needs him and he's in complete control
jokes.punchline: Mid-declaration, the sedative takes effect and he loses control of his bladder — the most literal possible refutation of his control claims
jokes.explanation: Structurally perfect: Michael's speech about control is literally interrupted by an involuntary bodily function. The sentence fragment '—I'm in con—' followed by the confused 'Am I peeing?' is beautiful construction. Thematically resonant (the control freak loses all control), physically funny, and highly quotable. Among the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Nurse's response to Michael peeing: 'I'm sorry. We gave you a little something to relax you. It may have taken the tingle out of your genitals.'
jokes.setup: Michael is confused about peeing involuntarily
jokes.punchline: The nurse's clinical description — 'taken the tingle out of your genitals' — echoes Gob's earlier 'dingle less tingle' in medical form, creating an accidental rhyme/callback
jokes.explanation: The callback to Gob's 'tingle' language is likely intentional — the whole episode plays with the word. Works on its own as a clinical understatement joke too.

jokes.text: 'I'm sorry to say this, but it's too late for me to do anything for your son.' / 'Let him keep talking.'
jokes.setup: The doctor says it's too late to do anything for 'your son' — meaning George Michael has already been assigned to a different doctor
jokes.punchline: Michael hears it as the doctor announcing his son's death; 'Let him keep talking' suggests Michael is either in denial or strategically gathering information — the ambiguity is the joke
jokes.explanation: A clean misdirection — the doctor's ambiguous statement sounds like a death pronouncement; 'Let him keep talking' is Michael at his darkest/funniest. The reveal (just a doctor reassignment) is appropriately anticlimactic. AD at its structural best.

jokes.text: Dr. Stein introduced as 'the best' — caveat: 'Well, only in the county'
jokes.setup: 'You're lucky. He's the best.'
jokes.punchline: 'Well, only in the county.' — instantly deflating the superlative
jokes.explanation: A quick, clean joke about the relativity of medical excellence. The economy is excellent — four words undercut the entire sales pitch. Slightly stock in structure but well-executed.

jokes.text: Tobias pivots from the medical discussion: 'Or it could be your colon. I'd want to get in there and find some answers.'
jokes.setup: Doctors are debating appendix vs. hernia
jokes.punchline: Tobias, posing as a doctor, chimes in with the colon as a third option and expresses inappropriate eagerness to go 'in there' — his never-extinguished desire to practice medicine (and get physical with patients) resurfaces
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Tobias's enthusiasm — 'I'd want to get in there' has the double meaning of medical and something more inappropriate, consistent with the show's running innuendo around Tobias's subconscious. Character-specific and consistent with seasons of established personality.

jokes.text: Michael uses the doctors-as-authority-figures as a lesson in deference, saying employees need to respect him 'just like the employees at the Bluth Company need to respect me' — while literally peeing himself
jokes.setup: Michael lectures about authority while sedated and apparently incontinent
jokes.punchline: The gap between what he's saying (respect authority) and his condition (can't control his own bladder) is the joke — ironic self-undermining
jokes.explanation: Thematically the richest joke in the hospital sequence — Michael's control obsession is being directly mocked by the narrative. The structural irony is very strong even if the specific line isn't highly quotable.

jokes.text: Dr. Stein: 'I'm going to put you on this machine to slow down your heart rate. Hopefully not too slow, because it's already dangerously slow as it is.'
jokes.setup: Dr. Stein explains the treatment plan
jokes.punchline: He casually mentions the procedure could kill Michael while noting Michael's heart is already dangerously slow — presented as an afterthought
jokes.explanation: Dark medical comedy done well — the casualness of 'hopefully not too slow' while acknowledging an already-dangerous condition is a clean dark joke. Dr. Stein is being established as a questionable doctor, which pays off later.

jokes.text: Michael, sedated, deferring to Dr. Stein: 'Whatever you think, Doctor. You see, he's the authority here. We need to respect him just like the employees at the Bluth Company need to respect me.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been railing about authority and control all episode.
jokes.punchline: Sedated and vulnerable, he articulates the episode's theme while not realizing he's making the opposite point — respecting someone else's authority while demanding his own.
jokes.explanation: A strong thematic comedy beat — Michael's character flaw illuminated in real time without self-awareness. The sedation provides perfect in-universe cover for the meta-commentary. His application of 'authority' to justify submitting to someone else's power is exactly the kind of irony the show does well.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Tony Wonder had startled the world by baking himself into a loaf of bread. He sold DVDs of it on his Web site, which many claimed suffered due to its pop-up ads.'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder is introduced as a famous rival magician
jokes.punchline: His signature trick (baking himself into bread) is presented straight; then the punchline is that his DVD sales underperformed because of pop-up ads on his website — a totally mundane internet grievance appended to a miraculous event
jokes.explanation: An extraordinary piece of absurdist writing. The structure is: miraculous supernatural achievement → commercial underperformance → banal internet reason. The descent from 'startled the world' to 'pop-up ads' is a perfect comedic anticlimax. The specificity of pop-up ads (a very 2005 concern) grounds the absurdism in a recognizable reality. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: Buster finally feels like 'a real person, not some deformed—' then screams when Gob notices his hook and calls him 'Elephant Man'
jokes.setup: Buster is having a moment of feeling normal and included
jokes.punchline: Gob's reaction to the hook ('Forgot about that thing, Elephant Man') immediately shatters Buster's dignity — the word 'Elephant Man' is specifically cruel for being both wrong and reductive
jokes.explanation: Gob's specific wrong reference ('Elephant Man' vs. 'Captain Hook') is the joke — he can't even be accurately cruel. The timing of the interruption mid-Buster-moment is well-structured. Character-specific cruelty that works.

jokes.text: George Sr. hears the employees need reassurance and responds with a triumphant 'Booyah!' — which refers to Tobias being sent to the office
jokes.setup: George Sr. wants someone to reassure the employees
jokes.punchline: The solution is Tobias, announced with Tobias's own earlier catchphrase 'Booyah' — the word has traveled from Tobias's résumé presentation to George Sr.'s crisis management
jokes.explanation: The callback gives 'Booyah' a new ironic context — it's now associated with a terrible plan rather than Tobias's own triumph. Rewards attentive viewers.

jokes.text: Buster's mechanical Halloween hand whirs and waves uncontrollably as an 'outfit' during the magic show setup
jokes.setup: Buster has been outfitted with a mechanical Halloween hand to replace his hook for the show
jokes.punchline: The hand has a will of its own, whirring and gesturing — a visual gag where the prosthetic is more animated than its owner
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy that doesn't fully translate in transcript form but the whirring, autonomous Halloween hand is a great physical gag. Character-integrated because only Buster's storyline creates this situation.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder whispers his trick to Buster: 'I folded it up into some squares, and I put it in a pouch under my shirt and then I pull it out' — then 'Keep it to yourself'
jokes.setup: Buster asks how Tony did the bread trick
jokes.punchline: Tony immediately and completely explains his secret to a stranger, then adds 'Keep it to yourself' — a joke about the banality of magic secrets and the absurdity of magicians' codes
jokes.explanation: A great joke about the gap between mystique and reality. The trick is boringly simple (folded bread in a shirt pouch), and Tony shares it instantly, undercutting the entire 'magicians only' mythology. 'Keep it to yourself' following complete disclosure is a perfect button.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder says 'I started out as a little W. Somehow I became a big one. I don't even know how it happened.'
jokes.setup: Tony is encouraging Buster that he'll improve over time
jokes.punchline: He describes his career trajectory in terms of letter size — 'little W' to 'big W' — which means nothing but is delivered with complete sincerity and introspection
jokes.explanation: A beautifully absurd inspirational speech that means literally nothing. The 'Wonder' in Tony Wonder starts with a W — so 'little W' to 'big one' is perhaps a growth metaphor using his own name, but delivered as if it's profound wisdom. The 'I don't even know how it happened' kicker is perfect: even he doesn't understand the metaphor. High quotability.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder gets poked in the eye by Buster's mechanical hand and screams 'My eye!'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder is trying to make Buster feel better about his mechanical hand
jokes.punchline: The mechanical hand immediately injures Tony — 'My eye!' — undercutting the warm moment
jokes.explanation: Good physical escalation — the hand that's been whirring threateningly finally finds a victim. Tony being the casualty is fitting because he was being magnanimous. Solid slapstick timing.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder's parting gift: 'Enjoy the Hanukkah cookie, man'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder has just had his eye poked and is departing
jokes.punchline: Despite the injury, he leaves with a cheerful Hanukkah cookie gift — the specificity of Hanukkah (not Christmas, not generic 'holiday') delivered in a casual 'man' is absurdly incongruous
jokes.explanation: The Hanukkah cookie is a small, perfect absurdist detail. It implies Tony Wonder pulls themed baked goods from his torso as a regular practice, and the 'man' casualness suggests this is how he lives — distributing holiday cookies from his person. Very AD.

jokes.text: Tobias addresses the employees: 'My name is Dr. Tobias Fünke and I'll be filling in for Michael, who is not sick.' [Nervous chuckle]
jokes.setup: Tobias is sent to reassure employees that everything is fine
jokes.punchline: He immediately undermines his own cover story by nervously clarifying Michael is 'not sick' — the chuckle signals he knows it's a lie
jokes.explanation: Tobias's inability to lie convincingly is a reliable character trait. 'Who is not sick' as an unprompted defensive clarification is textbook incompetent covering — immediately suspicious.

jokes.text: 'In fact, just today I saw a sliver of his buttocks and they're as fresh and firm as a Georgia peach, so not to worry.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to reassure employees about Michael's health
jokes.punchline: He uses having seen Michael's buttocks as medical evidence — and provides a detailed sensory review of their quality
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect Tobias line. It operates on multiple levels: it's medically meaningless, it reveals Tobias looked at his brother-in-law's buttocks (and assessed them with enthusiasm), and it's delivered with the gravitas of a clinical opinion. 'Fresh and firm as a Georgia peach' is an extraordinary phrase in this context. Maximum character integration — only Tobias says this. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: 'But before you all rise up and say, "Well, let's make this man our leader," well, I think that won't be necessary after you see this.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is addressing employees and about to show them a video
jokes.punchline: He preemptively stops the crowd from making him leader — which no one was going to do — revealing his delusional self-perception while appearing humble
jokes.explanation: Tobias's narcissism masquerading as self-deprecation. The phrase 'before you all rise up and say, let's make this man our leader' is a grandiose fantasy he's proactively managing. Strong character integration — Tobias alone would expect this reaction from a confused crowd. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Lindsay teaches George Michael to drive with the instruction 'Jam on it! It feels great, right?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay, who has never been a responsible adult, is teaching a teenager to drive
jokes.punchline: Her driving instruction is to 'jam on it' — aggressive acceleration as the lesson's first principle — with the enthusiasm of someone who has never worried about consequences
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's reckless approach to parenting distilled into one driving instruction. The 'It feels great, right?' confirms she's not teaching — she's just enjoying herself. Perfect character integration.

jokes.text: Gob says 'And now our magic trick.' Buster replies 'Illusion.' Gob: 'Silence, slave!'
jokes.setup: Gob announces their act
jokes.punchline: Buster corrects 'trick' to 'illusion' (a callback to Gob's own pedantry about the word in earlier seasons), only for Gob to shut him down as a 'slave' — he takes the correction when it's from others but not when from his own assistant
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and a perfect AD callback. For seasons, Gob has aggressively corrected people who say 'trick' instead of 'illusion.' Now Buster does the same correction — and Gob silences him with 'Silence, slave!' The hypocrisy is instant and total. Long-running gag subverted with maximum efficiency. Extremely quotable and has huge rewatch value for fans.

jokes.text: Buster hands Gob the sword 'It's a real sword, master.' The crowd applauds when they realize — Gob: 'We've got 'em. I'm gonna milk this for a minute.'
jokes.setup: Gob has asked for the 'trick sword' expecting a prop
jokes.punchline: Buster has actually brought the real Sword of Destiny; the crowd thinks it's part of the act and applauds; Gob, bleeding, decides to use their mistake as a performance beat
jokes.explanation: A great moment of escalating danger played for laughs. Gob recognizes the absurd situation and immediately pivots to exploitation — 'I'm gonna milk this for a minute' while panting from injury is a perfect character beat. The applause for an accidental dangerous moment is a joke about audience gullibility.

jokes.text: Gob: 'May I have the trick sword, please?' Buster: 'It's a real sword, master.' [Light applause from audience]
jokes.setup: Gob asks for the fake sword to perform a safe illusion.
jokes.punchline: Buster — who has been calling it 'the Sword of Destiny' — informs him it's a real sword. The audience applauds, thinking this is part of the act.
jokes.explanation: The escalating tension of a real sword being used in a fake-sword trick is well-constructed, and the audience's misreading of the situation (light applause at the reveal) is a clever layering — the 'audience within the show' thinking it's theatrical while we know it's genuine danger. Structure is strong.

jokes.text: Gob, preparing to milk the real-sword tension: 'We've got 'em. I'm gonna milk this for a minute.' [Panting]
jokes.setup: The audience is responding to the real sword reveal.
jokes.punchline: Gob, who is apparently terrified and panting, nonetheless decides to 'milk it' — performer instinct overriding self-preservation.
jokes.explanation: This is a perfect Gob moment. Despite clearly being in distress (panting), his showman ego immediately takes over: 'milk this for a minute.' The character integration is exceptional — Gob's narcissism is the only thing capable of overriding genuine fear. The gap between his emotional state and his professional calculation is the source of the laugh.

jokes.text: Buster's mechanical hand grabs Gob's sword arm and won't let go; Gob screams 'My hand! My hand!' and the crowd applauds thinking it's the trick
jokes.setup: Gob is performing the sword illusion
jokes.punchline: The mechanical hand malfunctions (or Buster botches it) and Gob is genuinely injured; the crowd cheers, treating real injury as entertainment
jokes.explanation: The episode's escalating sword injury pattern pays off here. The crowd's applause for Gob's genuine suffering is a dark irony: the illusion is real, and reality is being taken as illusion. Solid physical comedy.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder: 'My brother's widow. It's — [bleeps] — up.'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder is dismissing his female assistant Candida before talking to Gob
jokes.punchline: He casually reveals the assistant is his brother's widow — and his assessment is a bleeped expletive — the bleeping suggests a very specific word is being censored in an otherwise casual disclosure
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy via offhand family revelation. The bleep is funnier than whatever word was used — it implies the most specific and emphatic word for the situation. Tony Wonder's breezy attitude toward clearly complicated personal dynamics is consistent with his character.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder tells Gob about his planned DVD 'Use Your Illusion' — reveals 'some band has got the rights to that title. So I'm thinking, like, Use Your Illusion II.'
jokes.setup: Tony pitches his DVD title 'Use Your Illusion'
jokes.punchline: The title is taken — by Guns N' Roses — so he proposes the sequel title 'Use Your Illusion II,' apparently unaware that also exists, or aware and not caring
jokes.explanation: A beautifully layered pop culture joke. 'Use Your Illusion' and 'Use Your Illusion II' were both released simultaneously by Guns N' Roses — so the 'solution' has the same problem. The joke rewards knowledge of the albums and works even without it as straightforward absurdism. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder's DVD plan includes literary allusions: 'like you know Poe or something. Don't do Poe, 'cause I'm doin' Poe.' / 'What about Chaucer?' / 'Just not Poe, okay?'
jokes.setup: Tony discusses putting literary allusions in the 'Use Your Illusion: Allusion' DVD
jokes.punchline: Tony is territorial about Poe specifically — he's claimed the Poe allusion — and his creative framework for a magic DVD involves protecting literary territory
jokes.explanation: Tony Wonder as an artist who takes literary territory seriously is an absurd character beat. The Poe protectiveness is specific and weird enough to be funny. The wordplay on 'illusion/allusion' (Use Your Allusion with an A) is a nice setup for this.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder's parting gift to Gob is a Subway Sub Club card — 'It still needs three stamps.'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder dramatically offers Gob 'a free round of drinks' — the classic generous magician gesture after a big moment
jokes.punchline: He hands over a Subway Sub Club loyalty card with three stamps still needed — essentially nothing
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection and anticlimax. The setup ('courtesy of Tony Wonder') promises a grand gesture; the Sub Club card with three stamps still needed is the most bathetically specific possible disappointment. 'That's a magician.' as the button is a perfect straight-man response. One of the episode's strongest jokes structurally.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I was going to videotape that hop-on.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is reporting on George Michael's first driving experience
jokes.punchline: Lucille's priority was not her grandson's safety but capturing the comedic moment on tape — her response to a potential accident is regret over a missed video opportunity
jokes.explanation: Lucille Bluth is one of television's great comic monsters, and this line is pure Lucille: casually cruel, self-interested, and funny. The line is only eight words and tells you everything about her character. High character integration — only Lucille would say this about her grandson's near-miss.

jokes.text: George Michael reports on Lindsay's driving lesson: 'She's still trying to get the stairs unwedged from the emergency room overhang.'
jokes.setup: Michael is angry Lindsay taught George Michael to drive
jokes.punchline: George Michael's report reveals the lesson ended with the stair car's stairs lodged in a hospital overhang — the full disaster, delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: The information density here is extraordinary — the stair car got stuck, it happened at the emergency room (where Michael already is), and Lindsay is still there trying to fix it. All of that in one sentence. George Michael's casual delivery is perfect. Multiple callback layers.

jokes.text: Doctor reveals the appendix wasn't inflamed after all: 'Once I got in there, the appendix wasn't so inflamed. D'oh.'
jokes.setup: Michael went in for an appendectomy
jokes.punchline: The appendix was fine — unnecessary surgery performed — delivered with a Homer Simpson 'D'oh' by the allegedly best doctor in the county
jokes.explanation: The 'D'oh' — a casual acknowledgment of medical malpractice — is the joke. It's dark (unnecessary surgery) and the casualness of 'D'oh' as a response to cutting someone open unnecessarily is a perfect dark-comedy beat. The episode has been building Dr. Stein as incompetent; this confirms it.

jokes.text: Second surgery planned for an infection from the first surgery: 'I say we go in there Dr. Stein-style and cut it out. I want to show these interns what the best doctor in Southern California can do.'
jokes.setup: An abscess has developed from the unnecessary appendectomy
jokes.punchline: Dr. Stein pitches a second surgery with the enthusiasm of a showman — 'Dr. Stein-style' — and uses Michael as a teaching specimen, upgrading his own credentials from 'best in the county' to 'best in Southern California'
jokes.explanation: The credential inflation from 'county' to 'Southern California' is a callback that tracks Dr. Stein's growing delusion/confidence. 'Dr. Stein-style' as a brand for reckless surgery is a great character touch. The escalation of Michael's medical misadventures is well-structured.

jokes.text: 'And you are peeing again.' — as Michael is making an important point about authority
jokes.setup: Michael is making a passionate speech about respecting authority
jokes.punchline: He is interrupted with the observation that he's peeing again — the sedative has returned; his body contradicts his authority claims for the second time
jokes.explanation: The callback to the peeing moment is deployed with perfect timing — Michael is arguing for his own authority when his body betrays him again. The repetition of the exact same humiliation while he's making the exact same argument is structurally impeccable.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael realized he was going to wake up to nothing but trouble.' — as the news reveals George Sr. is in Iraq
jokes.setup: Narrator suggests Michael realized bad things are coming
jokes.punchline: The actual trouble (FBI identifying George Sr. from Tobias's reassurance tape, linking the attic to Saddam Hussein's houses) is revealed immediately — 'nothing but trouble' is an understatement
jokes.explanation: The multiple layered callbacks pay off simultaneously: Tobias's reassurance tape inadvertently exposed George Sr.'s location; the attic design links to Saddam Hussein (an ongoing show joke about the Bluth Company's Iraqi construction contracts). Dense but earned.

jokes.text: FBI news broadcast plays the tape — which ends with Tobias in turban saying 'This is my demand. Booyah!'
jokes.setup: The FBI found footage of George Sr. that inadvertently incriminates him
jokes.punchline: The tape George Sr. made also includes Tobias in a turban saying 'This is my demand. Booyah!' — making it look like a hostage/terrorist video
jokes.explanation: A masterpiece of AD's multi-thread payoff structure. 'Booyah' — introduced as Tobias's résumé enthusiasm — has now traveled through George Sr.'s tape and emerged as what the FBI interprets as a terrorist demand. Every element (turban, attic that looks like Saddam's house, 'This is my demand') is a callback paying off simultaneously. This is the kind of writing that made AD legendary. Approaches a 9.5 on craft.

jokes.text: 'Booyah!' [on the news broadcast, from George Sr.'s demand tape]
jokes.setup: George Sr.'s demand tape has been broadcast on the news as a terrorist communication.
jokes.punchline: 'Booyah!' — his sign-off on the tape — plays on the news broadcast, making a goofy exclamation the apparent rallying cry of an international fugitive.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best structural joke. 'Booyah' was first used by Tobias — incongruously, when presenting his résumé. George Sr. adopted it for his demand tape. Now it plays on national news as the apparent war cry of an international fugitive/terrorist suspect. A single nonsense word has traveled from a job application to a news broadcast about Saddam Hussein's houses in the span of one episode. The construction is impeccable — three plants, one devastating payoff. Economy is maximal — the word does all the work. This is Arrested Development at its most itself.

jokes.text: Gob: 'This DVD is the destiny the sword has chosen for me. I wonder where he is.' Tony Wonder: 'Did somebody say Wonder?'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder has been hiding in a dumbwaiter waiting for someone to say the word 'wonder'
jokes.punchline: Gob accidentally triggers the entrance with 'I wonder where he is' — Tony has been waiting over 20 minutes for this moment
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's cleverest bits. The narrator reveals Tony has been in the dumbwaiter for 20+ minutes waiting for the word 'wonder' — and the trigger is the commonplace phrase 'I wonder where he is.' The commitment to the bit (20 minutes hiding) makes it funnier. The narrator's revelation adds a layer of absurdism that recontextualizes the entrance. Excellent construction.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Actually, he'd been hiding inside the dumbwaiter for over 20 minutes, waiting for someone to use the word wonder.'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder has just appeared seemingly from nowhere
jokes.punchline: The revelation that this was a 20-minute commitment to a very specific trigger word — hidden in a cramped dumbwaiter — reframes the dramatic entrance as an elaborate, slightly pathetic piece of theater
jokes.explanation: The narrator as comedy engine is at its best here. The specific '20 minutes' detail is what makes it — not 'a while' or 'some time' but 20 minutes specifically, in a dumbwaiter, waiting for a common word. The gap between Tony's theatrical self-image and the reality (cramped, long wait, triggered by accident) is a perfect comedic juxtaposition.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder: 'Oh, hey, you've got a piece of lettuce on your shoulder.' [produces it] 'Ta-da! Part of the trick.'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder is transitioning into a business meeting.
jokes.punchline: He can't resist turning a casual observation into a magic reveal — or alternatively, the lettuce is leftover from his earlier bread/dumbwaiter trick.
jokes.explanation: The lettuce as a residual prop from the dumbwaiter trick — 'part of the trick' being a retroactive claim — is a neat bit of physical continuity comedy. Tony Wonder's compulsive need to turn everything into a trick, even a casual social observation, is consistent character comedy.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder apparently produces a piece of lettuce from nowhere and says 'Ta-da!'
jokes.setup: Tony notices Buster has a piece of lettuce on his shoulder
jokes.punchline: Tony picks it off and announces it as a magic trick — 'Ta-da!' — performing nothing while claiming to perform something
jokes.explanation: Tony Wonder's ability to brand mundane actions as magic is a consistent and funny character trait. Removing lint and saying 'Ta-da!' is exactly what a showman with no self-awareness does. Good economy.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I grant him magic sanctuary.' Tony: 'That's fine. I second the sanctuary. But with a clause of silence.'
jokes.setup: Gob wants Buster included in the DVD deal negotiations
jokes.punchline: Both Tony and Gob speak in invented magicians' legal terminology — 'magic sanctuary,' 'second the sanctuary,' 'clause of silence' — as if there is an actual binding magical governance system
jokes.explanation: A great piece of absurdist world-building. The magicians have their own legal/political system with formal motions and clauses, treated completely seriously by both parties. 'Clause of silence' as a way to silence Buster is both a worldbuilding joke and a convenient dramatic tool. The specificity of the invented governance is the joke's strength.

jokes.text: Tony now pitches the DVD title 'Use Your Illusion' — now 'Allusion' with an A — and wants them to put in a literary allusion
jokes.setup: Tony originally said 'Use Your Illusion II'; now he's re-titled it 'Use Your Allusion' to sidestep the Guns N' Roses issue
jokes.punchline: The new title is a homophone wordplay — 'Allusion' instead of 'Illusion' — and now requires actual literary allusions be included in a magic DVD
jokes.explanation: The Illusion/Allusion switch is a clever wordplay escalation of the earlier joke. It creates a new absurd requirement (the DVD must now contain Poe or Chaucer) while solving the naming problem in the most ridiculous way possible. The callback adds depth.

jokes.text: Gob dramatically declares he and Buster are inseparable: 'My assistant is my partner. I need him.' — immediately followed by Tony asking 'For what, Buster?'
jokes.setup: Gob makes a speech defending Buster as his indispensable partner
jokes.punchline: Tony immediately and reasonably points out that Buster's value is unclear — 'For what, Buster?' — the implication being Buster contributes essentially nothing
jokes.explanation: A clean deflation of Gob's sentiment. The question is obviously valid since Buster has been nothing but a liability. The joke works on the absurdity of Gob's partnership with his incompetent brother.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder on Buster's mechanical hand dance moves: 'Check out those moves you're doin', man. They're in their own orbit.'
jokes.setup: Buster's mechanical hand is moving uncontrollably
jokes.punchline: Tony interprets the uncontrollable arm spasms as impressive dance moves — 'in their own orbit'
jokes.explanation: A solid character joke — Tony Wonder sees artistry in mechanical malfunction. 'In their own orbit' is a genuine comic phrase. The recontextualization of disability as talent is the joke's engine.

jokes.text: Tony on his assistant Tiagra: 'Look at Tiagra, my girl. Don't look at her. 'Cause you look at her and it affects her in a way that I don't feel comfortable with. Or go for it. I don't care, whatever. I'm just sayin' it's your world.'
jokes.setup: Tony references his assistant Tiagra in passing
jokes.punchline: He issues contradictory commands about looking at her, offers a confusing reason (it affects her in a way he doesn't feel comfortable with), then immediately rescinds all instructions — 'it's your world'
jokes.explanation: Tony Wonder's stream-of-consciousness non-sequitur about Tiagra is a great character monologue. The contradictions — look/don't look/whatever — create an absurdist rhythm. 'It's your world' as a finale to a contradictory command is perfectly written. Also, 'Tiagra' as a name (Viagra/tiger hybrid) is a background joke.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder slips on something behind the scenes and screams: 'Ow! Darryl, I told you to move that [bleeps] chair 10 times! And clean the [bleeps] dumbwaiter while you're at it!'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder has just made a dramatic exit
jokes.punchline: His polished persona immediately collapses — he's screaming at a stagehand named Darryl and specifically referencing the dumbwaiter he was hiding in
jokes.explanation: The mask coming off — Tony Wonder is a diva who yells at crew members — is a great character reveal. The dumbwaiter callback ('clean the [bleeps] dumbwaiter') is a perfect detail: his theatrical suffering in that box was unnecessary, and now he's furious about the conditions. The contrast between 'enjoy the Hanukkah cookie, man' and 'Darryl!' is maximum.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder: 'Clean the [bleep] dumbwaiter while you're at it!'
jokes.setup: Tony is berating his unseen assistant after his chair injury.
jokes.punchline: The dumbwaiter — in which he hid for 20 minutes — is apparently dirty enough to need cleaning, adding an undignified dimension to his theatrical entrance.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the dumbwaiter — now revealed as a dirty one he chose to hide in — retroactively makes his theatrical entrance even more pathetic. The specific anger about a dirty dumbwaiter suggests this is a recurring problem in his staging routine.

jokes.text: Buster's wistful reaction to Tony's missed trick opportunity: 'Oh, I was really hoping for a graham cracker. That would have been a good spot for a trick.'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder has slipped and screamed at Darryl rather than performing a trick
jokes.punchline: Buster's only takeaway from the chaos is disappointment about the missing graham cracker — a snack preference that reveals his childlike priority system
jokes.explanation: A perfect Buster line. His response to the chaos is snack-focused — the graham cracker is a hilariously specific, infantile priority. 'That would have been a good spot for a trick' as a critical analysis of Tony Wonder's missed opportunity is beautifully innocent. Maximum character integration: only Buster thinks this way.

jokes.text: Doctor: 'We got the abscess, but I think we might have left some snippers in there.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if the second surgery went well
jokes.punchline: The surgeon casually mentions he may have left surgical scissors (snippers) inside Michael's body — presented with 'I think' uncertainty, as if he's not sure
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Dr. Stein's incompetence to 'left tools inside the patient' is dark and funny. 'I think' as the qualifying uncertainty is perfect — he genuinely doesn't know. 'Snippers' as the term is also a casual-sounding word for a serious surgical instrument left in a body cavity.

jokes.text: Dr. Stein on the missing snippers: 'It's not very likely, but one of the assistants having taken them is even less likely. Unless I left them in my scrubs.'
jokes.setup: Michael is alarmed about potential surgical tools left inside him
jokes.punchline: Dr. Stein works through the probability: tools in the patient unlikely, taken by an assistant even less likely, then realizes they might just be in his pocket — a mundane alternative that doesn't reassure anyone
jokes.explanation: The logic chain is wonderfully wrong: if they're in his scrubs that means he operated without them, which is also bad. The 'or' framing treats both possibilities as roughly equivalent problems when they're not. Good comedic structure.

jokes.text: 'Tobias said he's handling it with the FBI and the employees have everything under control.' / 'No, that's the worst thing possible.'
jokes.setup: Lucille tries to reassure Michael
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately identifies 'Tobias handling it' as catastrophic — correctly, given Tobias's track record throughout the episode
jokes.explanation: A functional joke that works because the episode has established Tobias's failures thoroughly. Michael's immediate alarm is validated by everything we've seen. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Tobias addresses the press: 'Michael Bluth is not... in Iraq.' — with a hesitation
jokes.setup: Tobias is handling the FBI/news situation on Michael's behalf
jokes.punchline: His press statement is the bare minimum denial — 'Michael Bluth is not... in Iraq' — delivered with a hesitation suggesting he's not entirely sure this is true
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'in Iraq' suggests Tobias is improvising and the hesitation reveals doubt. It's a minimal statement that raises more questions than it answers. Good character comedy.

jokes.text: Michael arrives to find the office empty, catastrophizing — then discovers the employees have solved everything by moving floors
jokes.setup: Michael has been insisting he's needed and everyone would fall apart without him; he rushes to the office expecting disaster
jokes.punchline: The employees solved the budget crisis themselves (leased the top floor, moved down a floor) while Michael was hospitalized — they're celebrating; his control obsession was the obstacle
jokes.explanation: The structural irony is strong — the solution was Ted's idea from the beginning, the man Tobias (under Michael's authority) threw out of the office. The theme of the episode (control is the problem) pays off here. Not a joke in the traditional sense but strong ironic structure.

jokes.text: Ted reveals the employees leased the top floor, moved down, and solved the financial problem without Michael. 'We did it. We did it!' [Cheering]
jokes.setup: Michael has been insisting he must be in control or everything collapses.
jokes.punchline: The employees solved the problem themselves — and the resolution is the exact thing Michael rejected when Ted suggested it earlier in the episode.
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff of Ted's solution — which Tobias literally ejected him from the office for suggesting — is satisfying. The irony is threefold: (1) Michael's absence enabled the solution, (2) the solution was Ted's original idea, (3) Michael's insistence on control was the obstacle all along. 'We did it!' is calibrated to be cheerful in a way that stings Michael.

jokes.text: Gob on stage: 'What are you doin'? I'm Gob Bluth. I'm the real magician. This man is only the assistant.'
jokes.setup: Buster and Gob are about to perform the real Sword of Destiny trick
jokes.punchline: Gob breaks character and announces to the audience that he's actually the magician, not the assistant — revealing the hidden identity scheme mid-act
jokes.explanation: Gob's inability to suppress his ego even within his own plan is perfect character comedy. He designed the reveal to happen at this moment, but the delivery — 'I'm Gob Bluth' with the confidence of a man who should be famous — is self-undermining given the Magicians' Alliance backstory.

jokes.text: Buster hands Gob the sword — blade first. 'Handle first. Handle first.' [Screams] — Gob's fingers are cut off
jokes.setup: Buster is supposed to hand Gob the sword handle-first for the trick
jokes.punchline: Buster hands it blade-first; Gob's repeated screamed instruction ('Handle first!') is not heeded and he apparently loses fingers
jokes.explanation: The episode-long sword injury escalation pays off: the Sword of Destiny has been cutting Gob progressively throughout (hand, earlier injury) and now takes fingers. The audience applauding is a callback to the earlier real-sword-treated-as-trick moment. 'Handle first. Handle first.' as a panic instruction is very funny. Dark payoff to a carefully constructed running threat.

jokes.text: Buster hands Gob the real Sword of Destiny blade-first. 'Handle first. Handle first.' [Screams] 'Oh, my fingers! He cut off my fingers!'
jokes.setup: Gob asks for the sword; Buster passes it the wrong way.
jokes.punchline: Gob's fingers are cut off — or severely injured — by his own assistant handing him a sword blade-first. The audience applauds.
jokes.explanation: The callback layers are dense here: (1) the Sword of Destiny has already drawn blood from Gob once, (2) Buster's own hand situation makes him an inherently dangerous person to hand a sword to, (3) 'handle first, handle first' repeated in panic is perfect structural escalation, (4) the audience applauds again — the second time they've interpreted disaster as art. The detail 'he cut off my fingers' being bleeped and then met with audience applause is the episode's second-best physical comedy payoff.

jokes.text: 'I'm not kidding! He cut off my [bleeps] fingers!' — audience continues applauding
jokes.setup: Gob has just had his fingers cut off
jokes.punchline: The audience continues to applaud and shout 'More! More!' treating genuine mutilation as great entertainment — the illusion is real but being taken as illusion
jokes.explanation: The final beat of the magic show sequence — reality and illusion have fully inverted. Gob is injured and screaming; the audience is delighted. 'Guy is good. Damn good.' from an audience member while Gob screams is a perfect ironic coda.

jokes.text: 'I didn't know we had taquitos.' — audience member during Gob's screaming
jokes.setup: Gob is screaming that his fingers have been cut off
jokes.punchline: An audience member, apparently unaware of the crisis, is pleasantly surprised to discover taquitos are available at the venue
jokes.explanation: A masterpiece of comic juxtaposition and economy. Five words delivered during maximum crisis. The audience member's priorities (taquitos over a man losing fingers) is perfectly absurdist. The contrast between Gob's 'I'm not kidding!' and 'I didn't know we had taquitos' is one of the episode's funniest moments. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Michael decides to 'give up some authority' and lets George Michael drive — George Michael immediately runs over Michael with the car
jokes.setup: Michael makes a growth decision — he'll relinquish control, starting with letting George Michael drive
jokes.punchline: George Michael immediately runs him over — the first act of relinquished control results in the worst possible outcome for Michael
jokes.explanation: A structurally perfect joke and a thematic punchline for the entire episode. Michael's arc about control ends with him being run over the moment he gives any up. The narrator's setup — noting the 'more responsive gas pedal' — foreshadows the hit beautifully. The universe consistently punishes Michael both for controlling and for not controlling.

jokes.text: Dr. Stein on Michael's leg: 'I've shortened your calf muscles to theoretically increase your jumping ability.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been hit by a car; Dr. Stein is treating his leg injury
jokes.punchline: Dr. Stein's treatment for a car-related leg injury is shortening calf muscles — a completely inappropriate procedure — with the qualifier 'theoretically' suggesting this is unproven medical science
jokes.explanation: A career-high Dr. Stein line. 'Theoretically' is the key word — it admits the procedure is experimental while presenting it as a treatment. The jumping ability upgrade is completely unrelated to a car accident and reveals Dr. Stein has his own agenda. 'But if it works, I could be the best in the world' is a stunning character reveal — he's testing on patients. The credential escalation (county → Southern California → world) completes.

jokes.text: Dr. Stein: 'But if it works, I could be the best in the world.'
jokes.setup: Dr. Stein explains his experimental calf-shortening procedure
jokes.punchline: His motivation is revealed: he's testing theories on patients to advance his own career — 'the best in the world' completing the credential arc from 'county' to 'Southern California' to 'world'
jokes.explanation: A perfect three-act joke: 'best in the county' → 'best in Southern California' → 'best in the world.' The escalation of Dr. Stein's ambition matches his escalating malpractice. 'If it works' confirms Michael is a guinea pig. Compact, character-specific, structurally elegant. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I want the other doctor back.'
jokes.setup: Dr. Stein has revealed he experimentally shortened Michael's calf muscles
jokes.punchline: Michael's response is to request the previous doctor — who also performed unnecessary surgery and left snippers in him — as the lesser of two evils
jokes.explanation: Five words that perfectly encapsulate Michael's situation: the solution he's requesting (the first doctor) is also a problem. The fact that the alternative is preferable despite leaving tools inside him says everything about how bad Dr. Stein is. Very quotable.

jokes.text: [On the next Arrested Development] Gob's middle finger and index finger are revealed to have been switched in reattachment
jokes.setup: Gob's fingers are being reattached after being cut off
jokes.punchline: The fingers are reattached in the wrong order — middle and index switched — meaning Gob will permanently be flipping people off when trying to point
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly constructed teaser joke. The visual consequence of the switched fingers (permanent involuntary obscene gesture) is both dark and perfectly suited to Gob's character — a man who is constantly offensive will now be physically incapable of not being offensive. The doctor's casual 'Nice job, Frank' to himself is a great button. Extremely high rewatch value.

jokes.text: FBI interprets George Sr.'s message tape as a 'terrorist training film' — the footage is Tobias in karate gear shouting 'You're not half the man he was!'
jokes.setup: FBI found the tape turned in by a 'rabid and disgruntled employee' (Starla, who has actual rabies)
jokes.punchline: The tape contains Tobias doing karate and shouting — which the FBI interprets as a terrorist training film, and the narrator confirms: 'This kid's got moves.'
jokes.explanation: Multiple threads converge: Starla (with actual rabies — a callback) turned in the tape; Tobias's karate is mistaken for terrorism; the narrator's 'This kid's got moves' treats FBI evidence as a talent evaluation. The 'rabid and disgruntled employee' is also a layered joke — Starla is literally rabid. Dense, rewarding, and very AD.

jokes.text: FBI footage reveal: 'We think it's a terrorist training film.' Cut to: George Sr. yelling at Michael while Michael does push-ups. 'You're not half the man he was!'
jokes.setup: The FBI has been analyzing the demand tape. They find something else on it.
jokes.punchline: The 'terrorist training film' is George Sr. verbally abusing Michael during what appears to be a workout — completely mundane but visually damning out of context.
jokes.explanation: The 'terrorist training film' reveal as footage of abusive parenting is a sharp dark comedy beat. 'You're not half the man he was!' as a terrorist training slogan is both funny and deeply sad — it's genuine Bluth family dysfunction recontextualized by federal surveillance. The juxtaposition of 'terrorist training' and 'push-up encouragement from an abusive father' is the kind of dark structural irony AD does at its best. 'This kid's got moves' from the FBI agent is the tag that seals it.

tmdb_overview: Gob and Buster team up for some magic tricks with the help of Buster's fake hand, but when Tony Wonder, a rival magician, starts to mentor them, somebody gets the finger. Meanwhile, Michael is forced to delegate some work when he's hospitalized and Lindsay teaches George Michael how to drive using the staircar.
insight: Deadpan escalation carries a solid 80.9 score across 61 jokes in 23 minutes.
